fact_summary: A noun names a person, place, thing or idea; a pronoun stands in for a noun or noun phrase so that it need not be repeated; and an adjective describes or limits a noun or pronoun. Correct identification depends on the work a word performs in its sentence, because the same written form can belong to different word classes in different contexts. Pronouns must refer clearly to an antecedent, while adjectives normally answer questions such as what kind, which one, or how many.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the NIOS parts-of-speech lesson; only stable definitions and sentence functions were retained.

fact_summary: A verb forms the core of a clause and expresses an action, occurrence or state; auxiliaries combine with a main verb to build tense, aspect, voice or modality. An adverb can modify a verb, adjective, another adverb or a whole clause and may express manner, place, time, frequency or degree. A conjunction connects words, phrases or clauses: coordinating conjunctions join units of equal status, while subordinating conjunctions introduce a dependent clause and show its relation to a main clause.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the NIOS grammar lesson and limited to the word-class distinctions used in competitive-exam questions.

fact_summary: English tense choices combine time reference with aspect. Simple forms present actions, habits, states or completed events without emphasizing internal duration; continuous forms present an activity as ongoing or temporary; perfect forms relate an earlier situation to a later reference point; and perfect continuous forms combine prior duration with that later point. The surrounding time expression and intended meaning, not the verb ending alone, determine which form is appropriate in context.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the NIOS tense section; the summary preserves the form-and-use distinctions without inventing exceptions.

fact_summary: The current CBSE secondary English curriculum expressly requires learners to use the sequence of tenses correctly in different communication contexts. Tense consistency means choosing verb forms whose time relationships agree across main and subordinate clauses rather than changing tense mechanically. A shift is justified when the time frame or meaning changes; an unjustified shift makes the chronology unclear. Reported speech, conditional clauses and descriptions of successive events are common contexts in which this relationship must be checked.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Structures (Grammar) and Vocabulary learning outcomes in the official 2026-27 curriculum.

fact_summary: When direct speech is reported from a past reporting point, verb forms commonly move to a corresponding past form so that the time relationship remains clear: a present form may become past, and a past form may become past perfect. The change is not automatic when the statement remains universally true or the reporting context preserves the original time reference. Pronouns and time or place expressions must also be adjusted according to the new speaker, listener, place and reporting time.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the tense-change guidance and examples in NIOS Senior Secondary English Book 1.

fact_summary: The current CBSE English Communicative specification assesses grammar through contextual gap filling, editing or omission, sentence reordering and sentence transformation. The stated grammar areas include tenses, modals, subject-verb concord, reported speech, noun, adverb and relative clauses, and determiners. Error detection should therefore test each sentence within its context, identify the rule governing the suspect form, and distinguish an actual grammatical violation from a merely unfamiliar but valid construction.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official grammar syllabus and test-type list; no unsupported error category was added.

fact_summary: CBSE's current secondary learning outcomes require clear and coherent writing with appropriate grammar, vocabulary and organisation, followed by review, editing and improvement. Its grammar programme reinforces accuracy through editing tasks, sentence transformation and contextual exercises. A valid sentence improvement must preserve the intended meaning while correcting the specific defect; it should also keep agreement, tense, reference, word order and register consistent with the surrounding sentence rather than replacing a correct phrase merely for stylistic preference.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the writing and grammar outcomes of the official 2026-27 Class IX English curriculum.

fact_summary: Parallelism requires two or more coordinated sentence elements to share the same grammatical or conceptual structure. Items in a series, paired comparisons and correlative constructions should therefore match in form: nouns with nouns, finite clauses with finite clauses, or corresponding infinitive and participial phrases. A faulty series mixes forms and obscures the relationship among its members. Correction restores the common pattern while preserving the original meaning and the grammatical role of each element.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the University of North Carolina Writing Center's institutional parallelism guide.

fact_summary: A dangling modifier occurs when an introductory phrase has an implied subject different from the subject of the main clause, producing an illogical or ambiguous meaning. An introductory modifying phrase should normally be followed immediately by the noun or noun phrase it describes. The sentence can be repaired by making that intended doer the main-clause subject or by rewriting the introductory phrase as a clause with its own explicit subject, then checking that the revised sentence states the intended relationship.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Australian Government Style Manual explanation and correction method.

source_title: Quick Guide: Common Misspellings and Word Confusion
fact_summary: Appropriate usage depends on both spelling and grammatical function. The Style Manual distinguishes commonly confused forms such as accept and except, advice and advise, affect and effect, appraise and apprise, and ascent and assent by their meanings and parts of speech. A usage question should be solved by identifying the role required by the sentence and then selecting the word whose definition fits that context; similarity of sound or spelling is not evidence that two forms are interchangeable.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Australian Government quick guide's definitions and examples.

fact_summary: Concise writing uses no more words than are needed to make the meaning clear. Redundancy occurs when a word or phrase repeats information already expressed or adds no necessary meaning; removing it should leave the proposition complete and unambiguous. Sentence improvement therefore favours a shorter option only when it preserves all essential relationships and emphasis. Brevity alone is not enough: deleting an article, connector, qualification or repeated noun that resolves ambiguity can make a sentence worse rather than better.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Australian Government handbook's concise-writing guidance.

source_title: Spelling: Top 50 Spelling Mistakes in English
fact_summary: Cambridge's exam-focused list records frequent spelling traps and the letter patterns that distinguish the correct forms. Examples include accommodation with double c and double m, address with double d and double s, beginning with double n, and necessary with one c and double s. These forms should be learned as complete word patterns and verified in context; pronunciation alone is unreliable because English spelling can retain doubled consonants, silent letters and unstressed vowels that are not obvious in speech.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against Cambridge English Grammar Today's publisher-authored exam spelling list.

source_title: Quick Guide: Common Misspellings and Word Confusion
fact_summary: A reliable spelling check uses the dictionary headword and also considers hyphenation, capitalisation and plural form. The government guide warns that similar-looking or similar-sounding words can both be correctly spelt yet wrong for the sentence, so spelling questions must separate orthographic errors from word-choice errors. The forms accept and except, advice and advise, complement and compliment, and principal and principle are distinguished by meaning and grammatical role rather than by sound alone.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Australian Government spelling and word-confusion quick guide.

fact_summary: Ambidextrous is an adjective for a person able to use both hands equally well. It can also describe an object designed for convenient use by either left-handed or right-handed people, and in an extended sense can describe someone able to perform different kinds of work well. For the standard one-word-substitution prompt 'able to use both hands equally well,' ambidextrous is the exact lexical match; ambivalent and ambiguous have unrelated meanings and are not substitutes.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary entry and restricted to its stated senses.

fact_summary: Bibliophile is a formal countable noun meaning a person who loves or collects books. It supplies a precise one-word substitution for 'a lover or collector of books.' The word denotes the person, not the collection, place or occupation: a library is a collection or place for books, a librarian works with library services, and a bibliography is a list of works. Competitive-exam options should therefore be compared by both meaning and grammatical category.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary entry.

fact_summary: Omniscient is a formal adjective meaning having, or seeming to have, unlimited knowledge. It is the direct one-word substitution for 'all-knowing.' The adjective must be distinguished from omnipotent, meaning all-powerful, and omnipresent, meaning present everywhere. Its related noun is omniscience. In narrative terminology an omniscient narrator is outside the story and knows everything about its characters and events, which illustrates the same core meaning without changing the word's grammatical class.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Cambridge learner and academic dictionary senses.

fact_summary: Ad hoc describes something made, arranged or happening for a particular purpose or immediate need rather than planned in advance. It commonly appears before a noun, as in an ad hoc committee, and in the phrase on an ad hoc basis. The expression does not mean permanent, routine or universally applicable. In an exam sentence, the surrounding idea of a special, temporary response to a specific situation is the decisive context for choosing this expression.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against Cambridge's learner, academic and business dictionary senses.

fact_summary: Bona fide is an adjective meaning real or genuine rather than false. It can qualify a person, organisation, claim or transaction whose authenticity is being asserted, as in a bona fide company. The related plural noun bona fides refers to credentials or evidence of good faith, but it is not grammatically interchangeable with the adjective. In foreign-expression questions, bona fide should be selected for the idea of genuineness, not for temporary arrangements or merely actual control.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary entry.

fact_summary: De facto describes a situation that exists in fact or practice even if it is not formally established or legally recognised. It contrasts with de jure, which concerns what exists by law or official right. A person may therefore be a de facto leader by exercising actual control without holding the formal title. The expression should be chosen when the sentence contrasts practical reality with official status; it does not simply mean genuine, temporary or unchanged.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against Cambridge's English and business dictionary senses.

fact_summary: The status quo is the existing condition or state of affairs at a particular time, especially in social or political matters. To maintain or preserve the status quo is to keep that existing arrangement unchanged, while to challenge it is to seek change. The expression names the condition itself and is normally used with the definite article. It should not be confused with de facto, which marks what exists in practice despite a different formal position.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary entry.

fact_summary: In their common uses, affect is a verb meaning to influence or cause a person or thing to change, while effect is a noun meaning the result of an influence. The sentence frame helps decide: a subject can affect an object, and an action can have an effect on something. Less common specialist senses exist, but competitive-exam questions normally test this basic verb-noun contrast. The correct choice must also receive any required inflection, such as affected or effects.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against Cambridge English Grammar Today's usage distinction.

fact_summary: Advice is an uncountable noun for an opinion or recommendation about what someone should do; advise is the corresponding verb meaning to give such a recommendation. Standard usage therefore permits some advice or a piece of advice, not an advice, while a person advises someone to act. The grammatical slot identifies the required form: a determiner or adjective may introduce the noun, whereas tense, agreement and infinitive patterns apply to the verb.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against Cambridge English Grammar Today's usage distinction.

fact_summary: CBSE's current English Communicative scheme uses gap filling to test grammar in context across tenses, modals, subject-verb concord, reported speech, clauses and determiners. A grammar-based cloze blank must be solved by reading the entire sentence and nearby sentences, identifying the grammatical function of the gap, and applying all relevant constraints such as agreement, tense sequence, article choice and clause structure. An option that fits semantically but violates the sentence pattern cannot be correct.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official 2026-27 grammar content and gap-filling assessment specification.

fact_summary: The current curriculum develops vocabulary through contextual reading and writing, asks learners to infer unfamiliar meanings from context and reference tools, and requires appropriate words and expressions. In a vocabulary cloze, a candidate must fit the passage's meaning, collocation, register and grammatical form. Near-synonyms are not automatically interchangeable: the surrounding words may require a conventional word partnership, a particular preposition, or a level of formality that only one option supplies.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official vocabulary learning outcomes and contextual-exercise guidance.

source_title: English Language and Literature Class X (2026-27)
fact_summary: The current CBSE reading specification assesses conceptual understanding, decoding, analysis, inference, interpretation and vocabulary, and includes case-based factual passages with verbal or visual information. A factual-comprehension answer must be traceable to an explicit statement, detail, relationship, table or chart in the supplied material. It should not import outside knowledge or convert an author's qualified claim into an absolute one; dates, quantities, comparisons and referents must match the passage exactly.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official 2026-27 reading-comprehension competencies and passage types.

fact_summary: CBSE's current secondary outcomes require learners to infer meanings and conclusions from textual evidence and to understand an author's perspective, tone and style. An inference must follow from clues in the passage even when it is not stated word for word; it cannot rely only on personal opinion. Tone is the writer's attitude as signalled by diction, emphasis, qualification, imagery and sentence pattern. The best answer accounts for the passage as a whole and avoids labels contradicted by its language.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the reading outcomes in the official Class IX 2026-27 English curriculum.

fact_summary: The current CBSE English Communicative assessment includes sentence reordering or sentence transformation in context. Rearranging parts requires construction of a complete grammatical clause: the subject and finite verb must agree, modifiers must sit next to what they describe, complements must follow the governing word, and connectors must link compatible units. After forming a grammatical sentence, the candidate must confirm that its information order and reference fit the supplied context, because more than one isolated sequence may look plausible.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official 2026-27 contextual sentence-reordering assessment specification.

fact_summary: NIOS explains that sequence markers identify the order of steps or events, using forms such as first, next, later, then, afterwards, before, finally and lastly. In paragraph sequencing, these markers supply explicit chronological constraints, while pronouns, repeated key terms, cause-effect links and general-to-specific development supply additional constraints. A correct order should let each reference point backward or forward clearly and should form one continuous progression rather than a set of individually grammatical sentences.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the NIOS sequence-marker and process-writing guidance.

fact_summary: The current curriculum requires coherent organisation, identification of main ideas and supporting details, and writing with a clear beginning, middle and conclusion. An opening sentence normally introduces the subject without depending on an unexplained pronoun, contrast or previously mentioned detail. A closing sentence normally completes the development by giving a result, conclusion or final implication rather than introducing a new unsupported topic. These roles must still be confirmed against the paragraph's actual logical and referential links.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the reading, writing and organisation outcomes of the official 2026-27 curriculum.

fact_summary: Sequence markers make temporal and procedural relationships explicit: first opens an ordered series, next and then continue it, before and afterwards relate events, and finally or lastly signals the concluding step. In a para-jumble these words are constraints, not decoration. They must agree with the events described and with other relations such as cause, contrast and example. A sequence is coherent only when the connector at each boundary accurately describes the relationship between the linked sentences.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the NIOS explanation and examples of sequence markers.

fact_summary: The current CBSE English Core framework treats cohesion and coherence as parts of fluent communication and evaluates organisation and logical connection between ideas. Coherence is the intelligible development of meaning across the passage; cohesion is created by visible links such as reference words, repetition, substitution and connectors. A para-jumble solution must satisfy both: adjacent sentences should link linguistically, and the full order should develop one stable topic without contradiction, unexplained reference or an abrupt logical gap.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official fluency, cohesion, coherence and organisation criteria.
